schools.summary: Theophylact: constant prayer the remedy for the coming trials; Augustine: if perseverance prevailed with an unjust judge, how much more with God.
The meaning prefixed (a rare key); 'not to faint' (enkakein), a Luke/Paul word (Gal 6:9; 2 Cor 4:1) — not to lose heart.
Calvin: perseverance in prayer rare and difficult, but the evidence of faith; Henry: all God's people are praying people.

A 'hard-boiled' judge fearing neither God nor man — no higher authority restrained, no shame moved him; reflecting the corrupt justice of Galilee/Peraea; the parable argues by contrast, not likeness, to God.
The widow defenceless, the law's special care (Isa 1:17; Exod 22:22); 'avenge me' better 'do me justice' (not revenge but vindication); 'came oft' (imperfect) — her only resource is persistence.
His long refusal (imperfect, 'continued refusal') measures his indifference; he yields not from justice but self-interest; a deed outwardly right from a wholly selfish root (Clarke).
'Weary me' (hypopiaze) a boxing metaphor — 'stun me,' leave a bruise (cf 1 Cor 9:27); he relents from relief not right; her persistence, not her cause, conquers him — importunity wins.

'The unjust judge' = 'the judge of unrighteousness' (characterising genitive, as 'steward of unrighteousness' Luke 16:8); the Lord begins the application — if such a man yielded, how much more the merciful God (Clarke).
The a fortiori: if a wicked judge yields, much more will God avenge his elect who cry day and night; the delay is not indifference but longsuffering forbearance (cf 2 Pet 3:9).
Gill: the 'elect' God will surely vindicate; the bearing-long is part of his mercy.
'Speedily' = suddenly, surely, when his hour strikes; the closing question expects a negative — faith will be rare, love cold (Jas 5:8); some tie the 'coming' to the vengeance on Jerusalem.
Calvin: men sink because they neglect the remedy of prayer; the question forearms against the offence of seeing all in confusion.
Augustine: faith the gift of the humble, not the proud; Theophylact: pride disturbs the mind and is a contempt of God, ascribing to self what is God's.
Two faults: trusting their own works for righteousness and 'setting all others at naught' (exoutheneo); the Pharisee no hypocrite but sincerely self-trusting (Wesley).
Calvin: arrogance the most dangerous disease, fixed in the marrow; we must come to God with abasement.
'Went up' suited the Temple's height on Moriah; Pharisee and publican vividly suggestive — the pattern of repute and of reproach; same place and posture, opposite hearts.
Calvin: the two seem to show the same ardour of piety, yet are exceedingly unlike.
Theophylact: he names first what he lacked, then what he had; Chrysostom: not enough to despise all men, he must attack the publican — true thanks does not reproach others.
Calvin: blamed not for boasting of free-will but for trusting his works for reconciliation; his thanksgiving was really self-praise.
Robertson: he struck an ostentatious attitude, a complacent soliloquy with his own soul.

themes: 'I thank thee that I am not'

schools.summary: The law required one fast a year (Lev 16:29); the Pharisees added two a week, made meritorious as voluntary; he tithed all his income — the prayer asks nothing, only displays.
Calvin: he claims to do more than the law required, like monks boasting works of supererogation.
'Afar off' from the crowd, no ostentation; refused to lift his eyes; smote his breast — tokens of deep distress; owns himself 'a sinner' and casts himself on mercy.
Calvin: not a prescribed ritual but the tokens of humility — acknowledging sin, self-condemning, anticipating God's judgment.

themes: 'God be merciful to me a sinner'

schools.summary: 'Justified' = accounted/treated righteous (perfect = abiding); the publican accepted, the Pharisee not; the 'house' the test of worship's reality; the maxim repeats Luke 14:11.
Calvin: to justify is to stand before God as righteous — the publican accepted, the Pharisee totally rejected.
Theophylact: a lesson of humility by his own example, receiving the children; Augustine: brought to the Saviour to be saved; Ambrose excuses the disciples — they acted from love of their Master.
Luke's 'babes' (brephe, infants) stronger than Mark/Matthew's 'little children'; Henry: fitly follows the publican, confirming the humble are accepted.
Jesus calls the children to himself and rebukes the disciples' rebuke (as Mark 10:14); 'of such is the kingdom' — children and the childlike are its subjects (Wesley).

The child is the model for seekers (not the adult for the child); to receive the kingdom 'as a little child' is to receive it with simple, dependent trust, without claim or merit (cf 1 Pet 1:14) — the gate to entrance.
Bede: he asks openly by what works he may merit eternal life; Ambrose: he should have said 'Good God,' not merely 'Good Master.'
'By doing what shall I inherit?' — the lawyer's question (Luke 10:25); he thought some one act could win eternal life; Henry: a hopeful sign that one of rank cared for his soul.

Not a disclaimer of his goodness but a probe — why use a word belonging fully to God? Lightfoot: meant to lead the ruler from a careless compliment to a true confession of the Messiah (closer to Mark 10:18).
Titus of Bostra: know God first, then seek; Theophylact on the order; Ambrose: honouring parents includes giving bountifully, not mere respect.
The second-table commandments cited (Exod 20:12; Rom 13:9); Luke with Matthew omits Mark's 'defraud not' — the law as the mirror of his need.
'From my youth up' a detail shared with Mark 10:20; he claims, sincerely but blindly, to have kept the whole second table — measuring himself by the letter, finding nothing wanting.
'One thing still fails thee' — a searching diagnosis; sell all, give to the poor, follow me; the demand probes where his heart is bound (treasure in heaven, Matt 6:19-20).
Wesley: the one thing was to love God more than mammon; the command to sell all was his particular cure — not commanded to all, but to love God above the world.
Luke's word for sorrow between Matthew's and Mark's vivid 'lowering' (Mark 10:22); 'very rich' = 'great possessions' — his wealth the measure of his grief, the test finding him out.

themes: near the kingdom yet held back

schools.summary: Theophylact: 'hardly,' not 'impossibly' — the rich may reap a heavenly reward, but with difficulty, for wealth grips the heart; Bede: he who despises earthly affections shall receive far greater.
The peril of riches (1 Tim 6:9); the difficulty is the heart's clinging, not wealth itself.

A current proverb for the impossible; Luke's 'needle' (belone, a surgeon's needle) a classical word — a sign of his culture; the variant 'cable'/'camel' noted but the camel the stronger reading; the impossibility is the point.
The hearers assumed wealth a mark of divine favour, so 'who then can be saved?' — the saying overturned their reckoning; Luke's phrasing may suggest others present besides the disciples.

The saving paradox: salvation impossible to man, possible with God (Jer 32:17; Luke 1:37); God can break the grip of gold — confirming the camel-saying meant the humanly impossible; salvation wholly God's work.
Peter not boasting but reacting from consternation, seeking reassurance; 'we have left all' — homes, trades, families ('our own,' ta idia) — set against the rich ruler who would not.

The catalogue of dearest ties (house, parents, brethren, wife, children); Luke omits 'lands'; the decisive phrase 'for the kingdom of God's sake' (cf Deut 33:9) — the sacrifice counts only when made for Christ.

'Manifold more' (pollaplasiona) now — chiefly spiritual: peace, joy, the family of faith (Gill) — and life everlasting hereafter (Rev 2:10); Luke keeps the promise simple, omitting the thrones, persecutions, first/last.
Gregory: he forewarns the troubled disciples of both Passion and Resurrection; the suffering fulfils the prophets (against heretics).
Chrysostom: told apart that habituated by expectation they might bear it (Isa 53:5); Cyril of Jerusalem: he came willingly, lest the world perish in its sins.
Robertson: taking the Twelve apart (Matt 20:17), a special point of explaining his death.
Nearly as the other Gospels, but 'spitefully entreated' is peculiar to Luke; fulfilled when the chief priests delivered him to Pilate and the soldiers (Acts 3:13) — the foretold shame of the Passion.

'The third day' (Matt 20:19; Mark's 'after three days' the same sense); scourging fulfilled by Pilate before crucifixion — death and resurrection foretold in order, the Lord controlling his own narrative.
Clarke: the resurrection foretold (Hos 6:2) — his rising the pledge, token, and cause of ours.
Luke says it three times — they kept not perceiving, it was 'hid from them' (cf Luke 9:45); the cause their fixed hope of a temporal conqueror; the physician dwells on the psychological cause.
Calvin: a strange stupidity, but the time of understanding was not yet — the truth would open after the events and the Spirit.
Gregory: the carnal disciples brought to a miracle to strengthen faith; Ambrose: the blind man a type of the Gentiles receiving the lost light; the Lord did not walk without doing good (Theophylact).
The harmony addressed — two Jerichos (old/new Roman); Mark names Bartimaeus (10:46), Matthew mentions two (20:30); selective focus, not contradiction.

debates.question: One blind man or two, entering or leaving Jericho?

sides.position: reconciled by the two Jerichos and the Evangelists' selective focus

schools.summary: 'A multitude' (no article) conveys the vague impression of many footsteps; the imperfect 'inquired' — he repeatedly asked what it meant, the soul in darkness pressing toward the light.
They tell him 'Jesus of Nazareth passeth by' (present tense retained) — the lowly title of his reproach; the report meets his seeking and kindles faith into action.
Gill: the prophet of Nazareth, famed for doctrines and miracles, said to be the Messiah, was just then passing by.
'Son of David' (huie Daueid) confesses Jesus as Messiah (Matt 9:27); 'he cried' (eboesen) shouted aloud — bold yet humble, pleading only for mercy.

'They which went before' (a part of the crowd, not the disciples) tried to silence him; he 'cried so much the more' (Mark 10:48) — the rebuke redoubled his cry, faith that obstacles cannot quench (cf Ps 141:1).
'Stood' — Jesus stopped (Mark 10:49); on his way to the cross he halts the procession and commands the beggar brought near, overruling the crowd's rebuke with his welcome.
Christ draws out the request he already knew; Luke's 'Lord' (kyrie) for Mark's 'Rabboni,' a higher reverence matching 'Son of David'; the man seeks not alms now but his sight.

'Thy faith hath saved thee' — better 'made thee whole' (as Mark), the bodily healing carrying a deeper sense of salvation (cf Luke 7:50; 17:19); cured by a bare word, without means, his faith honoured.

Instant cure by the word; 'followed' (imperfect, began to follow, Mark 10:52); the effect on man and crowd peculiar to Luke, who loved such glorifying of God (Luke 5:26; Acts 3:8); the common people discern what the doctors would not (Clarke).
